Acne.desc: Acne is a common skin condition where hair follicles get clogged with oil and dead skin cells, leading to pimples, blackheads, and whiteheads, mainly on the face, chest, and back.
Acne.treatment: Use salicylic acid or benzoyl peroxide face wash, keep skin clean, avoid oily products, and consult a dermatologist if severe.

Actinic_Keratosis.desc: Actinic Keratosis appears as rough, scaly patches caused by long-term sun exposure and is considered a precancerous condition.
Actinic_Keratosis.treatment: Apply sunscreen regularly, avoid sun exposure, and treatments include cryotherapy or medicated creams.

Benign_tumors.desc: Benign tumors are non-cancerous skin growths that do not spread to other parts of the body and are usually harmless.
Benign_tumors.treatment: No treatment is needed unless they cause discomfort; removal can be done via surgery or laser.

Bullous.desc: Bullous skin conditions cause large fluid-filled blisters due to autoimmune or allergic reactions.
Bullous.treatment: Treatment includes corticosteroids, immunosuppressive drugs, and proper wound care.

Candidiasis.desc: Candidiasis is a fungal infection caused by Candida species, commonly affecting moist areas like skin folds.
Candidiasis.treatment: Use antifungal creams or oral antifungal medicines and keep the area dry.

DrugEruption.desc: Drug eruption is a skin reaction caused by medications, resulting in rashes, redness, or itching.
DrugEruption.treatment: Stop the triggering medicine (with doctor advice) and use antihistamines or steroids.

Eczema.desc: Eczema is a chronic inflammatory condition causing dry, itchy, and irritated skin.
Eczema.treatment: Use moisturizers, avoid triggers, apply steroid creams, and maintain skin hydration.

Infestations_Bites.desc: This includes skin reactions caused by insect bites or infestations like mites or lice.
Infestations_Bites.treatment: Use antihistamines, soothing creams, or anti-parasitic medications.

Lichen.desc: Lichen planus is an inflammatory condition causing purple, itchy, flat bumps on the skin.
Lichen.treatment: Use corticosteroids, antihistamines, and immune-modulating medications.

Lupus.desc: Lupus is an autoimmune disease that can affect the skin, causing rashes especially on sun-exposed areas.
Lupus.treatment: Use sun protection, corticosteroids, and immunosuppressive drugs.

Moles.desc: Moles are small, dark skin growths formed due to clusters of pigment cells and are usually harmless.
Moles.treatment: No treatment needed unless changes occur; suspicious moles should be checked by a doctor.

Psoriasis.desc: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune disease that speeds up skin cell production, causing red, scaly patches.
Psoriasis.treatment: Use topical creams, phototherapy, and systemic medications.

Rosacea.desc: Rosacea causes redness and visible blood vessels on the face, sometimes with pimples.
Rosacea.treatment: Avoid triggers, use gentle skincare, and apply prescribed medication.

Seborrh_Keratoses.desc: Seborrheic keratoses are non-cancerous, wart-like skin growths that appear waxy or raised.
Seborrh_Keratoses.treatment: No treatment needed unless for cosmetic reasons; removal can be done easily.

SkinCancer.desc: Skin cancer occurs when abnormal skin cells grow uncontrollably and can become life-threatening if untreated.
SkinCancer.treatment: Early diagnosis is important; treatment includes surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy.

Sun_Sunlight_Damage.desc: Skin damage caused by prolonged exposure to sunlight, leading to pigmentation, aging, and lesions.
Sun_Sunlight_Damage.treatment: Use sunscreen, avoid sun exposure, and apply skin-repair treatments.

Tinea.desc: Tinea is a fungal infection (ringworm) that affects skin, hair, or nails.
Tinea.treatment: Use antifungal creams or oral medication.

Unknown_Normal.desc: This indicates normal or healthy skin with no visible disease detected.
Unknown_Normal.treatment: No treatment required; maintain good skincare routine.

Vascular_Tumors.desc: Vascular tumors are growths formed from blood vessels, such as hemangiomas.
Vascular_Tumors.treatment: Usually harmless; treatment includes laser therapy or surgery if needed.

Vasculitis.desc: Vasculitis is inflammation of blood vessels that can cause rashes, spots, or ulcers on the skin.
Vasculitis.treatment: Treatment includes corticosteroids and immunosuppressive drugs.

Vitiligo.desc: Vitiligo is a condition where skin loses its pigment, causing white patches.
Vitiligo.treatment: Light therapy, topical steroids, and depigmentation therapy.

Warts.desc: Warts are small skin growths caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV).
Warts.treatment: Salicylic acid, cryotherapy, or minor surgical removal.
